---
title: "AI Basics with AK"
subtitle: "Season 03 - Introduction to Statistics"
format:
  revealjs:
    incremental: false
    auto-stretch: true
    slide-number: false
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Episode 11 - Type I & Type II Errors and One-Sample Tests

## Recap: Episode 10 — Hypothesis Testing

::: {style="font-size: 70%;"}
| Concept | What It Means |
|---|---|
| **H₀ (Null Hypothesis)** | The default claim — nothing changed |
| **H₁ (Alternative)** | The interesting claim — something did change |
| **p-value** | Probability of seeing this data *if H₀ were true* |
| **α (significance level)** | Our threshold — usually 0.05 |
| **Decision Rule** | p < α → Reject H₀ |

> Last episode we learned *how* to test.
> This episode we ask: **what if we got it wrong?**
:::

## Every Decision Has a Risk

::::::: columns
:::: {.column width="50%"}
### The Problem

::: {style="font-size: 65%;"}
When we reject or fail to reject H₀ —

we are making a **decision under uncertainty.**

We never see the full truth.

We only see a sample.

And samples can mislead us.

So two kinds of mistakes are always possible.
:::
::::

:::: {.column width="50%"}
### The Two Mistakes

::: {style="font-size: 65%;"}
**Type I Error**
Rejecting H₀ when it is actually true.

*A false alarm.*

**Type II Error**
Failing to reject H₀ when it is actually false.

*A missed signal.*

Both are costly — just in different ways.
:::
::::
:::::::

## The Error Matrix

::: {style="font-size: 70%;"}

|  | **H₀ is True** | **H₀ is False** |
|---|---|---|
| **Reject H₀** | ❌ Type I Error (α) | ✅ Correct — True Positive |
| **Fail to Reject H₀** | ✅ Correct — True Negative | ❌ Type II Error (β) |

- **α** = P(Type I Error) = significance level you chose
- **β** = P(Type II Error) = depends on effect size, sample size, α

> You control α directly.
> β is influenced — not directly set.

:::

## Real World Cost of Each Error

::::::: columns
:::: {.column width="50%"}
### Type I Error — False Alarm

::: {style="font-size: 65%;"}
**Medical trial:**
Drug actually does nothing — but your test says it works.

→ Patients receive an ineffective drug

**Quality control:**
Machine is fine — but test flags it as faulty.

→ Production shuts down unnecessarily

**Cost:** Acting on something that isn't real.
:::
::::

:::: {.column width="50%"}
### Type II Error — Missed Signal

::: {style="font-size: 65%;"}
**Medical trial:**
Drug actually works — but your test misses it.

→ A beneficial treatment is never approved

**Quality control:**
Machine is broken — but test says it's fine.

→ Defective products reach customers

**Cost:** Failing to act when you should have.
:::
::::
:::::::

## Visualizing the Two Errors

```{python}
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from scipy import stats

x = np.linspace(-5, 9, 1000)

# Null and alternative distributions
mu0, mu1, sigma = 0, 3, 1.5
alpha = 0.05
z_crit = mu0 + stats.norm.ppf(1 - alpha) * sigma  # one-tailed critical value

y_null = stats.norm.pdf(x, mu0, sigma)
y_alt  = stats.norm.pdf(x, mu1, sigma)

fig = go.Figure()

# Null distribution
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=x, y=y_null, mode='lines',
    line=dict(color='steelblue', width=2.5),
    name='H₀ Distribution (μ=0)'
))

# Alternative distribution
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=x, y=y_alt, mode='lines',
    line=dict(color='darkorange', width=2.5),
    name='H₁ Distribution (μ=3)'
))

# Type I Error — right tail of null beyond critical value
x_t1 = x[x >= z_crit]
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=np.concatenate([[x_t1[0]], x_t1, [x_t1[-1]]]),
    y=np.concatenate([[0], stats.norm.pdf(x_t1, mu0, sigma), [0]]),
    fill='toself', fillcolor='rgba(220,50,50,0.4)',
    line=dict(color='rgba(0,0,0,0)'),
    name='Type I Error (α)'
))

# Type II Error — left tail of alternative before critical value
x_t2 = x[x <= z_crit]
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=np.concatenate([[x_t2[0]], x_t2, [x_t2[-1]]]),
    y=np.concatenate([[0], stats.norm.pdf(x_t2, mu1, sigma), [0]]),
    fill='toself', fillcolor='rgba(100,100,220,0.4)',
    line=dict(color='rgba(0,0,0,0)'),
    name='Type II Error (β)'
))

# Critical value line
fig.add_vline(x=z_crit, line_dash='dash', line_color='black',
              annotation_text=f'Critical Value = {z_crit:.2f}',
              annotation_position='top right')

fig.update_layout(
    title='Type I (α) and Type II (β) Errors',
    xaxis_title='Test Statistic',
    yaxis_title='Probability Density',
    height=460,
    showlegend=True
)

fig.show()
```

::: {style="font-size: 50%;"}
> Red = Type I Error (false alarm). Blue = Type II Error (missed signal). The critical value separates the two decision zones.
:::

## The Trade-Off Between α and β

::::::: columns
:::: {.column width="50%"}
### Lowering α (e.g., 0.01)

::: {style="font-size: 65%;"}
- Fewer false alarms ✅
- Harder to reject H₀
- More missed signals ❌
- β increases

**Use when:**
False alarms are very costly.

*Example: Approving a dangerous drug.*
:::
::::

:::: {.column width="50%"}
### Raising α (e.g., 0.10)

::: {style="font-size: 65%;"}
- More false alarms ❌
- Easier to reject H₀
- Fewer missed signals ✅
- β decreases

**Use when:**
Missing a real effect is very costly.

*Example: Cancer screening.*
:::
::::
:::::::

## Now We Test — One-Sample Tests

::: {style="font-size: 70%;"}

A **one-sample test** answers a simple question:

> *"Is the mean of my sample significantly different from a known or claimed value?"*

**Two tools depending on what you know:**

| Situation | Test to Use |
|---|---|
| σ (population std dev) is **known** | One-sample **z-test** |
| σ is **unknown** (use sample std dev s) | One-sample **t-test** |

In practice, σ is almost never known.

→ **t-test is what you'll use most of the time.**

:::

## z-test vs t-test — Quick Decision Guide

::: {style="font-size: 70%;"}

| Question | Answer | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Is σ known? | Yes | z-test |
| Is σ known? | No | t-test |
| Is n large (≥ 30) and σ unknown? | Yes | t-test (z ≈ t at large n anyway) |
| Is n small and σ unknown? | Yes | t-test — especially important |
| Not sure? | Always | **Default to t-test** |

> The t-test was invented *specifically* because we rarely know σ in practice.
> It is the workhorse of one-sample testing.

:::

# Thank You 🌊
